items.title: Introduction to Sliver-Weather
items.text:
What is sliver-weather.git?

Sliver-weather.git is a Docker Compose deployment designed for cybersecurity training and demonstration purposes. It showcases the Sliver C2 framework, utilizing a weather website as a cover application, nginx as a reverse proxy, and the Sliver C2 server for command and control operations.

items.title: Installation
items.text:
How do I install sliver-weather.git?

To install sliver-weather.git, you'll need to have Docker and Docker Compose installed on your system. Clone the repository using git clone https://github.com/your-repo/sliver-weather.git, then navigate into the cloned directory. Run docker-compose up -d to start the containers in detached mode.

git clone https://github.com/your-repo/sliver-weather.git
cd sliver-weather
docker-compose up -d
What are the system requirements for running sliver-weather.git?

Sliver-weather.git requires a system with Docker and Docker Compose installed. Ensure your system meets the minimum requirements for Docker and has sufficient resources (CPU, memory, and disk space) to run the containers smoothly.

items.title: Basic Usage
items.text:
How do I access the weather website?

Once the containers are up and running, you can access the weather website by navigating to http://localhost:8080 in your web browser. This will take you to the cover application, which is the weather website.

How do I interact with the Sliver C2 server?

To interact with the Sliver C2 server, you'll need to access the Sliver C2 web interface. This is typically done by navigating to http://localhost:8080/sliver in your web browser. You may need to authenticate using the credentials specified in the docker-compose.yml file or the Sliver C2 server configuration.

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
How do I configure the Sliver C2 server?

The Sliver C2 server configuration can be modified by editing the sliver.yaml file in the repository. This file contains settings such as the server's listening address, port, and authentication credentials. After making changes, you'll need to restart the Sliver C2 container using docker-compose restart sliver.

# sliver.yaml example
server:
  addr: 0.0.0.0
  port: 8080
auth:
  username: admin
  password: password123
Can I customize the weather website?

Yes, the weather website can be customized by modifying the HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files in the weather-website directory. You can also add your own weather data API or modify the existing one to suit your needs.

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
What if the containers don't start?

If the containers don't start, check the Docker Compose logs using docker-compose logs to identify any errors. Common issues include insufficient resources, incorrect configuration, or conflicts with other containers.

How do I troubleshoot Sliver C2 server issues?

To troubleshoot Sliver C2 server issues, check the Sliver C2 server logs, which are typically located in the sliver-logs directory. You can also increase the log level by modifying the sliver.yaml file to get more detailed output.

items.title: Contributing
items.text:
How can I contribute to sliver-weather.git?

To contribute to sliver-weather.git, fork the repository, make your changes, and submit a pull request. Ensure your changes are properly tested and follow the existing code style and conventions. You can also open issues to report bugs or suggest new features.

What are the guidelines for contributing code?

When contributing code, ensure you follow the existing code style and conventions. Use Markdown format for documentation, and include code examples where helpful. Test your changes thoroughly before submitting a pull request.

items.title: 📦 sliver‑weather — Your Personal Weather Companion
items.text:
sliver‑weather is a ready‑to‑run weather service that gives you up‑to‑date weather information wherever you are. It can be run locally on your computer or inside a Docker container, and it exposes a simple HTTP API that any app, script, or smart‑home device can talk to.

Tip: No programming knowledge is required to start using the service – just follow the steps below and you’ll have current conditions, forecasts, and alerts at your fingertips.

items.title: Table of Contents
items.text:
1. Getting Started 1.1. Run with Docker (recommended) 1.2. Run locally (Node.js)
  2. Core Features 2.1. Current Weather 2.2. 7‑Day Forecast 2.3. Weather Alerts & Warnings 2.4. Units & Language Settings 2.5. Location Search & Auto‑Complete
  3. Advanced Features 3.1. Public API Keys & Rate Limits 3.2. Webhooks for Push Notifications 3.3. Export Data (JSON/CSV)
  4. Configuration Options
  5. Common Use‑Cases & Example Commands
  6. FAQ & Troubleshooting

items.title: 1.1 Run with Docker  (the easiest way)
items.text:
# Pull the latest image from Docker Hub
docker pull ghcr.io/your‑org/sliver-weather:latest

# Start the container (replace <YOUR_API_KEY> with a weather‑provider key)
docker run -d \
  --name sliver-weather \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -e WEATHER_API_KEY=<YOUR_API_KEY> \
  ghcr.io/your‑org/sliver-weather:latest
  • The service will be reachable at http://localhost:8080.
  • All data is kept in memory; you can add a volume if you want to persist logs.

items.title: 1.2 Run locally (Node.js)
items.text:
Prerequisite: Node ≥ 18 and npm installed.

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/your‑org/sliver-weather.git
cd sliver-weather

# Install dependencies
npm ci

# Set your weather‑provider API key (you can also use a .env file)
export WEATHER_API_KEY=<YOUR_API_KEY>

# Start the server
npm start

The server starts on http://localhost:3000 by default (you can change the port with PORT=4000 npm start).

items.title: 2.1 Current Weather
items.text:
• What you get: temperature, humidity, wind speed, cloud cover, and a short description (e.g., “light rain”).
  • How to request: 
curl "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/current?lat=40.7128&lon=-74.0060"

Response (pretty‑printed JSON):

{
  "location": "New York, US",
  "temperature": 22,
  "unit": "C",
  "description": "Partly cloudy",
  "humidity": 58,
  "windSpeed": 5.2,
  "icon": "03d"
}

items.title: 2.2 7‑Day Forecast
items.text:
• What you get: daily high/low temperatures, precipitation probability, and a weather icon for the next 7 days.
  • How to request: 
curl "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/forecast?city=London"

Response snippet:

{
  "city": "London",
  "forecast": [
    { "date": "2026-03-03", "high": 12, "low": 5, "pop": 20, "icon": "01d" },
    { "date": "2026-03-04", "high": 14, "low": 6, "pop": 10, "icon": "02d" }
    // … 5 more days
  ]
}

items.title: 2.3 Weather Alerts & Warnings
items.text:
• What you get: national or regional alerts (e.g., storm warnings, flood notices).
  • How to request: 
curl "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/alerts?lat=34.0522&lon=-118.2437"

If there are no alerts, the service returns an empty array:

{ "alerts": [] }

items.title: 2.4 Units & Language Settings
items.text:
• Units: Celsius (default) or Fahrenheit.
  • Languages: English (default), Spanish, French, German, etc.

Add query parameters to any endpoint:

curl "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/current?city=Tokyo&units=F&lang=ja"

items.title: 2.5 Location Search & Auto‑Complete
items.text:
• What you get: a fast “type‑ahead” search that returns matching city names and coordinates.
  • How to request: 
curl "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/search?q=par"

Response:

{
  "results": [
    { "name": "Paris, FR", "lat": 48.8566, "lon": 2.3522 },
    { "name": "Parma, IT", "lat": 44.8015, "lon": 10.3279 }
  ]
}

items.title: 3.1 Public API Keys & Rate Limits
items.text:
• Free tier: 1,000 requests per day.
  • Pro tier: Unlimited (requires a paid key).
  • How to use a key: Include it as a header:
curl -H "X-API-Key: <YOUR_PUBLIC_KEY>" \
     "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/current?city=Berlin"

items.title: 3.2 Webhooks for Push Notifications
items.text:
You can have sliver‑weather POST a JSON payload to your own URL whenever a severe alert is issued.

# config/webhooks.yaml
alerts:
  - url: https://my‑home‑automation.local/notify
    events: ["storm", "flood"]

After adding the file, restart the service. When a matching alert appears, you’ll receive:

{
  "type": "storm",
  "location": "Miami, US",
  "severity": "high",
  "description": "Tropical storm approaching"
}

items.title: 3.3 Export Data (JSON / CSV)
items.text:
• Endpoint: /api/v1/export?format=csv&city=Oslo
  • Returns a downloadable file you can open in Excel or Google Sheets.
curl -OJ "http://localhost:8080/api/v1/export?format=csv&city=Oslo"
# -O saves the file with its original name (weather_oslo.csv)

items.text:

items.title: Prerequisites

items.title: Installation
items.text:
1. Clone the sliver-weather.git repository using the following command: 
    git clone https://github.com/JongoDB/sliver-weather.git
  2. Navigate to the project directory: 
    cd sliver-weather
  3. Build the Docker images using the following command: 
    docker-compose build
  4. Start the Docker containers using the following command: 
    docker-compose up -d
    This will start the containers in detached mode.

items.title: Quick Start
items.text:

To access the Sliver C2 server, navigate to http://localhost:8080 in your web browser. This will display the Sliver C2 server interface, where you can interact with the Sliver implant.

items.title: Troubleshooting Guide for sliver-weather
items.text:
(A friendly guide for non-developers)

Hi there! Weather apps are supposed to be calm and breezy—so if sliver-weather is giving you clouds instead of sun, let’s fix it together. Follow the steps in order; stop as soon as your problem is solved.

items.title: 1. Quick Checks (30-second sanity savers)
items.text: Check What to do ☑️ Internet Open a browser and visit google.com. Offline? Fix Wi-Fi first. ☑️ Docker running? Windows/Mac: look for the Docker whale icon in the tray. Linux: docker version in terminal. ☑️ Ports free? The app usually wants port 3000 (and 8080 for API). Close Zoom, Skype, or other local servers if they’re using those ports. ☑️ Copy-paste errors Retype any command manually—hidden characters from Slack/Teams love to break things.

items.title: 2.1 “docker: command not found”
items.text:
Cause: Docker isn’t installed or not in your PATH. Fix: - Windows/Mac: Install Docker Desktop - Linux: 

sudo apt update && sudo apt install docker.io docker-compose-plugin
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER   # then log out & back in

items.title: 2.2 “docker-compose: command not found”
items.text:
Cause: Modern Docker uses docker compose (space, no hyphen). Fix: 

docker compose up -d

items.title: 2.3 “permission denied while trying to connect to Docker daemon”
items.text:
Cause: Docker isn’t allowed for your user. Fix: 

sudo chmod 666 /var/run/docker.sock   # quick but temporary
# Better: add yourself to the docker group (see 2.1)

items.title: 3.1 “build failed: npm ERR! code EUSAGE” or similar
items.text:
Cause: Node modules inside the container are stale. Fix: 

docker compose down
docker compose build --no-cache
docker compose up

items.title: 3.2 “port 3000 is already allocated”
items.text:
Cause: Another app (or an old container) owns the port. Fix: 

docker ps -a               # list containers
docker stop <ID>           # stop the offender
docker rm <ID>             # remove it
Or change the port in docker-compose.yml: 
ports:
  - "3030:3000"

items.title: 3.3 Container exits immediately (STATUS=Exited (1))
items.text:
Cause: Missing environment variables or bad config file. Fix: 

docker logs sliver-weather-app-1   # last lines show the real error
Common missing vars: - OPENWEATHER_API_KEY (get one free at openweathermap.org) - LAT / LON (your city coordinates)

Add them to the .env file in the project root: 

OPENWEATHER_API_KEY=your_real_key_here
LAT=51.5074
LON=-0.1278
Then restart: 
docker compose down && docker compose up -d

items.title: 4.1 Web page shows “Cannot GET /” or blank white screen
items.text:
Cause: Frontend build didn’t finish. Fix: 

docker exec -it sliver-weather-app-1 npm run build   # inside container
Or rebuild everything: 
docker compose build --no-cache
docker compose up

items.title: 4.2 “Location not found” or wrong weather
items.text: Cause: Coordinates are reversed (longitude first?) or city name misspelled. Fix: - Check LAT and LON in .env (use positive for N/E, negative for S/W). - Reload the page with Ctrl + F5 to skip browser cache.

items.title: 4.3 High CPU / fans spinning
items.text:
Cause: Dev-mode hot-reload inside container. Fix: Switch to production image (if provided) or add: 

environment:
  - NODE_ENV=production
in docker-compose.yml, then restart.

items.title: 5. Updating to a Newer Version
items.text:
1. Save your .env file somewhere safe.
  2. Pull latest code: 
    git pull origin main
  3. Rebuild images: 
    docker compose down
docker compose build --no-cache
docker compose up -d

items.title: 6. Getting Help
items.text:
Where What to include 📄 GitHub Issues Paste the last 30 lines of docker logs, your OS, and the exact command you ran. 💬 Discord / Reddit A screenshot of the error + the .env file (hide your API key). 📧 Email maintainer Link to the repo + steps you already tried above.

Before opening a new issue: - Search closed issues for your exact error message. - Make sure you’re on the latest commit (git pull). - Strip passwords/keys before posting logs.

items.title: 7. One-Page Cheat-Sheet
items.text:
# 1. Clone
git clone https://github.com/<user>/sliver-weather.git
cd sliver-weather

# 2. Create .env
cp .env.example .env
# edit .env with your OPENWEATHER_API_KEY, LAT, LON

# 3. Run
docker compose up -d

# 4. View
open http://localhost:3000

# 5. Logs
docker logs -f sliver-weather-app-1

# 6. Stop
docker compose down

Still stuck? Don’t panic—weather apps are forgiving. Post your logs and we’ll get you sunny skies again!

items.title: Getting Started with sliver‑weather
items.text:
Welcome to sliver‑weather! This quick guide will walk you through installing the tool, running your first command, and getting the most out of it – all in just a few minutes.

Tip: If you run into any hiccups, feel free to ask for help on the project’s GitHub issues page or drop a comment in the community chat.

items.title: 1. Prerequisites
items.text:
Item Minimum Version Why you need it Git 2.20+ To clone the repository Python 3.8+ sliver‑weather is a Python CLI tool pip 20+ Python’s package installer

Windows users: You can install Git from git-scm.com and Python from python.org. Make sure to add both to your system’s PATH.

items.title: 2. Clone the Repository
items.text:
Open a terminal (or Command Prompt / PowerShell on Windows) and run:

git clone https://github.com/your-org/sliver-weather.git
cd sliver-weather

🎉 You’ve just downloaded the source code! The next step is to set up a virtual environment so that the tool’s dependencies don’t interfere with other Python projects on your machine.

items.title: 3. Create a Virtual Environment
items.text:
# Create a new virtual environment named "venv"
python -m venv venv

# Activate it
# • On macOS/Linux:
source venv/bin/activate
# • On Windows (cmd.exe):
venv\Scripts\activate.bat
# • On Windows (PowerShell):
venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1

You’ll know it’s active because your prompt will now start with (venv).

items.title: 4. Install the Dependencies
items.text:
pip install --upgrade pip
pip install -e .

The -e flag installs the package in “editable” mode, so any changes you make to the code will be reflected immediately.

items.title: 5. Verify the Installation
items.text:
Run the built‑in help command to see all available options:

sliver-weather --help

You should see something like:

Usage: sliver-weather [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

  A lightweight CLI for fetching weather information.

Options:
  --version  Show the current version and exit.
  --help     Show this message and exit.

Commands:
  get   Get the current weather for a location.
  forecast   Get a short‑term forecast.

If you see this, you’re ready to start using sliver‑weather!

items.title: 6. First Use: Get the Current Weather
items.text:
Let’s fetch the weather for your city. Replace YOUR_CITY with a real city name (e.g., London).

sliver-weather get "YOUR_CITY"

Example:

sliver-weather get "San Francisco"

You should see output similar to:

🌤️  San Francisco, US
Temperature: 18°C
Condition: Partly Cloudy
Wind: 5 km/h NE

items.title: 7. Quick‑Start: Forecast
items.text:
Want to see a short‑term forecast? Try:

sliver-weather forecast "YOUR_CITY" --days 3

Example:

sliver-weather forecast "Tokyo" --days 3

Output will list the weather for the next three days.

items.title: 8. Customizing the Output
items.text:
sliver‑weather supports a few handy flags:

Flag Description --units metric Show temperatures in Celsius (default). --units imperial Show temperatures in Fahrenheit. --format json Output data as JSON for scripting.

Example:

sliver-weather get "Berlin" --units imperial --format json

items.title: 9. Uninstalling
items.text:
If you ever want to remove the tool:

deactivate   # exit the virtual environment
pip uninstall sliver-weather

items.title: 10. Troubleshooting
items.text: Issue Fix sliver-weather: command not found Make sure the virtual environment is activated and that pip install -e . ran successfully. API errors The tool uses the OpenWeatherMap API. Ensure you have a valid API key set in the environment variable OWM_API_KEY. “Permission denied” On macOS/Linux, you may need to run chmod +x sliver-weather to make the script executable.

items.title: 11. Want to Contribute?
items.text: Great! Check out the CONTRIBUTING.md file in the repo for guidelines on how to submit pull requests, report bugs, or add new features.

items.title: 🎉 You’re All Set!
items.text:
You now have a fully functional weather CLI on your machine. Explore the commands, experiment with different locations, and enjoy the sunshine (or rain) data at your fingertips.

Happy coding! 🌞

items.title: Sliver-Weather – What It Is & Why You Might Use It
items.text: Sliver-Weather is a ready-to-run practice lab that bundles a harmless “weather” website with a hidden cybersecurity training tool called Sliver. In one click (okay, one command) it starts a miniature, self-contained internet on your own computer: a cute weather page anyone can visit, plus a powerful control panel security professionals use to learn how attackers operate. Everything runs inside Docker, so nothing touches your real system and you can delete it when you’re done.

items.title: Who It’s For
items.text:
• IT or security students who need a safe place to practice detecting and responding to intrusions
  • Teachers who want a classroom demo that looks real but is completely controlled
  • Companies running internal cyber-drills without risking production servers

If you’ve ever wished for a “practice range” that sets itself up in minutes and cleans itself up just as fast, this project is for you.

items.title: What You Can Do With It
items.text:
• Spin up a realistic-looking website (the weather app) that visitors see
  • Hide a Sliver command-and-control server behind the scenes
  • Let red-teamers generate “implants” (simulated malware) and see how they talk back
  • Let blue-teamers watch the traffic, learn the signs, and practice blocking or removing the implants
  • Reset the whole environment in seconds for another round of training

items.title: Quick Start
items.text:
1. Install Docker and Docker Compose
  2. Clone the repo, open a terminal in the folder
  3. Run docker compose up
  4. Browse to http://localhost for the weather site, or follow the on-screen instructions to reach the Sliver console
  5. When finished, press Ctrl-C and run docker compose down to wipe everything

No permanent changes, no complex installs—just a lightweight playground for learning modern attack-and-defense skills.

It interacts with the routing system for navigation and handles download logic which might be part of a larger ad management or user interaction system. The error handling falls back to opening a new window if something goes wrong. The SVG fallback image is used if the ad image fails to load.
